# Test Quality Review — `lib/features/sync/outbox/` + `test/features/sync/outbox/`

**Scope:** All four implementation files and five test files in the outbox feature.
**Generated by automated test-quality review (static analysis)**

---

## File inventory

| File | Lines | Has test? | Top issue |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lib/features/sync/outbox/outbox_daily_volume.dart` | 40 | Yes | None — well-sized value class |
| `lib/features/sync/outbox/outbox_processor.dart` | 355 | Yes | Clean; minor structural duplication between `_processSingle` / `_processBundle` |

| `lib/features/sync/outbox/outbox_service.dart` | **1961** | Yes | **4× over the 500-line guideline**; a single file mixes lifecycle, enqueue dispatch, merge logic, backoff, prune, watchdog, dbNudge |

| `test/features/sync/outbox/outbox_service_test.dart` | **7743** | — | **The single largest test file in the codebase;** 82 service constructions (48 `TestableOutboxService` + 34 `OutboxService`); inline mocks; real-wall-time wait; inline GetIt boilerplate |

---

## File size / split opportunities

- [ ] **[HIGH]** `lib/features/sync/outbox/outbox_service.dart` at **1961 lines** is the most over-limit file in scope (4× the 500-line soft ceiling). Clear seams exist:
  - **`OutboxEnqueueDispatch`** — `enqueueMessage`, `_priorityForMessage`, and all `_enqueue*` + `_prepare*` helpers (lines ~403–1930, roughly 1400 lines). This cohesive unit has zero dependency on `ClientRunner`, timers, or connectivity; it could be a standalone class accepting `SyncDatabase`, `JournalDb`, `VectorClockService`, `Directory`, `SyncSequenceLogService`.
  - **`OutboxLifecycle`** — constructor wiring, `_startRunner`, watchdog/prune timers, connectivity/login subscriptions, `sendNext`, `_drainOutbox`, backoff management, `dispose` (~lines 37–344 + 572–784, ~550 lines after the enqueue split).
  - `MatrixOutboxMessageSender` (lines 1952–1961) is trivially extractable.
  - Recommended split: create `lib/features/sync/outbox/outbox_enqueue_service.dart` + update `outbox_service.dart` to compose it. Mirror split in tests.

- [ ] **[HIGH]** `test/features/sync/outbox/outbox_service_test.dart` at **7743 lines** mirrors the impl bloat. Post-impl-split, the test file must also split: `outbox_enqueue_service_test.dart` (enqueue dispatch, merge, VC, sequence-log groups — the bulk) and a slimmer `outbox_service_test.dart` (lifecycle: watchdog, dbNudge, prune, sendNext, connectivity/login).

- [ ] **[MED]** `test/features/sync/outbox/outbox_processor_test.dart` at **1716 lines** is over the 500-line soft ceiling. Natural seams: (1) single-row path tests (lines 370–880), (2) bundle path tests (lines 1120–1716), (3) Glados property test (lines 481–601). The bundle group could move to `outbox_processor_bundle_test.dart`; however the one-file-per-source-file rule means all tests for one source file stay together — so the right fix is to trim boilerplate via `_TestBench` rather than splitting files.

- [ ] **[LOW]** `test/features/sync/outbox/outbox_retry_cap_db_test.dart` at 141 lines violates the one-test-file-per-source-file rule: it is an orphan (no matching source file named `outbox_retry_cap_db.dart`). Its two tests target `OutboxProcessor` (cap behavior, merge-race guard). They should be merged into `outbox_processor_test.dart` (the cap test) and `outbox_repository_test.dart` (the merge-race DB test), then the orphan file deleted.

---

## Test quality improvements

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **Inline mock classes violate the central-mock rule** (`test/mocks/mocks.dart`). Seven inline classes exist across outbox tests, none present in the central file:
  - `outbox_service_test.dart` lines 45–57: `MockUserActivityGate`, `MockOutboxRepository`, `MockOutboxMessageSender`, `MockOutboxProcessor`, `MockMatrixClient` (note: `MockMatrixClient` also exists in `mocks.dart:211` — the inline one is a duplicate), `MockCachedLoginController`.
  - `outbox_processor_test.dart` lines 17–19: `MockOutboxRepository`, `MockMessageSender`.
  - All six unique mocks should be added to `test/mocks/mocks.dart` and the inline definitions removed.

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **82 service constructions in `outbox_service_test.dart` with no `_TestBench`**. At least 48 `TestableOutboxService(...)` calls share the same 10-parameter boilerplate (`syncDatabase`, `loggingService`, `vectorClockService`, `journalDb`, `documentsDirectory`, `userActivityService`, `repository`, `messageSender`, `processor`). A `_TestBench` factory with a `build({activityGate, ownsActivityGate, sequenceLogService, saveJsonHandler, postDrainSettle})` method would remove ~400 lines of copy-paste and keep tests focused on the one parameter that varies. Currently every test that needs a non-default gate constructs a fresh service from scratch.

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **Inline `getIt` boilerplate** in `outbox_service_test.dart` at lines 370–453 (`getIt.isRegistered<Directory>()`, `getIt.unregister<Directory>()`, `getIt.registerSingleton<Directory>(...)`, `allowReassignment = true`). AGENTS.md mandates `setUpTestGetIt()` / `tearDownTestGetIt()` from `test/widget_test_utils.dart`. The service test bypasses this entirely and registers `Directory` manually in `setUp`/`tearDown`, creating a fragile pattern that can leave GetIt in a bad state if a test throws mid-setup. The fix: call `setUpTestGetIt()` and extend it to register the temp directory, or use its `overrides` mechanism.

- [ ] **[MED]** **Repeated Matrix-client setup boilerplate** (15 occurrences in `outbox_service_test.dart`). Every test that needs a `matrixService` independently creates `MockMatrixClient()`, `MockCachedLoginController()`, stubs `cached.stream`, `cached.value`, `client.onLoginStateChanged`, and `matrixService.client`. A file-level helper `_stubMatrixService({bool loggedIn = false})` → `MockMatrixService` would eliminate ~8 lines × 15 = 120 lines.

- [ ] **[MED]** **Smoke-only assertions in `outbox_daily_volume_test.dart`** lines 129–210: five static `test(...)` cases (`equality holds`, `inequality when date differs`, `inequality when totalBytes differs`, `inequality when itemCount differs`, `toString includes all fields`) are redundant — the Glados property at line 56 already covers all five dimensions with 120 runs. They add CI cost (5 extra test bodies) without adding coverage. Remove or merge into 1 concrete example.

- [ ] **[MED]** **Copy-paste permutation tests** in `outbox_service_test.dart` groups `'Message preparation'` (lines 5328–6306), `'Agent sequence log recording'` (lines 4830–5060), `'EntryLink sequence log recording'` (lines 4638–4828). Each group has 4–6 nearly-identical tests differing only in payload type (`SyncAgentEntity` vs `SyncAgentLink`, `recordSentEntry` vs `recordSentEntryLink`). These should use parameterized helpers or loops instead of duplicating the full test body.

- [ ] **[LOW]** `outbox_processor_test.dart` line 803 test `'send completing at exact timeout boundary doesnt race'` wraps `fakeAsync` in an `async` test but never `await`s the `fakeAsync` block — `fakeAsync` returns synchronously; the outer `async/await` is misleading. The pattern works (the inner lambda runs synchronously) but could confuse readers; the `async` keyword should be removed from the outer test signature.

---

## Generative (Glados) testing opportunities

All four Glados tests in scope carry `tags: 'glados'` and cover the key invariants well:

- `outbox_daily_volume_test.dart`: equality / hash / MB conversion — full coverage.
- `outbox_processor_test.dart` (`numRuns: 220`): bundle state machine across 6 input dimensions — excellent.
- `outbox_repository_test.dart` (`numRuns: 140`): batch mark semantics across selected/unselected rows — good.
- `outbox_service_test.dart` (`numRuns: 160`): priority classification stability — good.

One genuine gap:

- [ ] **[LOW]** **`VectorClock.mergeUniqueClocks` deduplication invariant** — the merge logic in `_enqueueJournalEntity`, `_enqueueEntryLink`, and `_enqueueAgentPayload` relies on `mergeUniqueClocks` being idempotent (applying the same VC twice leaves the list unchanged). This is pure logic with a clear algebraic property (`merge(merge(X, Y), Y) == merge(X, Y)`). A Glados test parameterized over arbitrary lists of `VectorClock` values would catch any regression in the merge implementation. This does NOT belong in the outbox service test — it belongs in the `VectorClock` unit tests.

No Glados candidates inside the outbox scope itself are missing: queue ordering / dedup / retry-backoff decisions are already covered by the processor's `_GeneratedProcessorScenario` test; the repository batch semantics are covered by `_GeneratedRepositoryBatchScenario`. Adding Glados to the service's enqueue dispatch would not add value because correctness there depends on DB I/O, not pure logic.

---

## Coverage / missing-behavior gaps

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **`_safePayloadFullPath` path-traversal boundary** — `outbox_service.dart` lines 391–401 contain a path normalization guard. The notification path-traversal test (`'enqueueMessage skips and logs when notification jsonPath escapes docs root'`, line 784) covers one variant, but `_safePayloadFullPath` is also called by `_enqueueNotification` (line 1486) when the `filePath` is already set. The case where `fullPath` equals `docsRoot` exactly (the `docsRoot != fullPath` false branch at line 397) is not explicitly tested.

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **`sendNext` login-gate stream emission path** — `outbox_service.dart` lines 718–729: `_loginGateEventsController.add(null)` fires only when (a) outside the startup grace, (b) not logged in, and (c) pending items exist. `outbox_service_test.dart` line 6661 tests that it does NOT emit inside the grace window, but there is no test that verifies it DOES emit once the grace window elapses and pending items are present. This is a missing positive assertion.

- [ ] **[MED]** **`_recordBackoff` at `delay <= Duration.zero` short-circuit** — `outbox_service.dart` line 573: `if (delay <= Duration.zero) return;`. No explicit test invokes `_recordBackoff(Duration.zero)` directly; the behavior is only covered indirectly through `sendNext` returning `Duration.zero` delays. A targeted unit test on `computeEnqueueDelay` when `_nextSendAllowedAt` is in the past would be cleaner.

- [ ] **[MED]** **`pruneSentOutboxItems` (non-chunked) in `DatabaseOutboxRepository`** — `outbox_repository.dart` line 190 — is tested in `outbox_repository_test.dart` lines 592–617 but the test only verifies delegation (mocked DB). The chunked variant gets both a delegation test and real-DB tests; the non-chunked variant has no real-DB test. Given this method is still public API, a real-DB round-trip test (insert sent rows, call prune, verify rows gone) would close the gap.

- [ ] **[MED]** **`computeEnqueueDelay` with `delay < Duration.zero`** — `outbox_service.dart` line 603: negative delay is clamped to `Duration.zero`. There is no test passing a negative duration to `computeEnqueueDelay`. It is `@visibleForTesting` so it can be tested directly.

- [ ] **[LOW]** **`_startRunner` prune path — `_startupPruneTimer` null-out** — `outbox_service.dart` line 300: `_startupPruneTimer = null` inside the timer callback is not observable in tests; covered only implicitly by the prune-fires-once test.

---

## Test execution speed opportunities

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **Real wall-clock wait in `outbox_service_test.dart` line 3356**: `await Future<void>.delayed(const Duration(milliseconds: 200))` is a real-time wait in the test `'runner logs gate wait when > 50ms'` (line 3322). The test comment at line 3351 correctly notes that `fakeAsync` cannot advance `DateTime.now()`. However this test uses real timers which makes it inherently flaky in slow CI environments (200 ms may not be enough for the 120 ms gate delay + runner scheduling). The implementation (`outbox_service.dart` line 234–237) uses `DateTime.now()` for wall-time measurement — if the clock were injectable or the threshold were parameterizable, this test could use `fakeAsync`. This is a known real-I/O exception per `test/README.md` policy, but the 200 ms real-sleep adds ~200 ms to every CI run of this shard. **Impact: ~200 ms per CI run, flake risk on slow agents.**

- [ ] **[HIGH]** **82 service construction calls with no `_TestBench`** — each `TestableOutboxService(...)` call creates a `MockUserActivityGate`, a mock `Directory`, and registers/unregisters `GetIt`. The construction itself is cheap, but the test-level teardown (`documentsDirectory.deleteSync(recursive: true)` + GetIt cleanup at lines 442–453) runs for every one of the ~130 test cases. A shared `_TestBench.create()` pattern (one fresh bench per test, but 90% of the boilerplate in the bench) would not reduce the number of constructions, but would make the teardown path cleaner and less error-prone. **Impact: low wall-time, high flake risk if a test fails mid-setUp and leaves GetIt dirty.**

- [ ] **[MED]** **Glados `numRuns: 220` in `outbox_processor_test.dart` line 483**. The processor scenario has 6 dimensions with small enumerations (rowCount 0–4 = 5 values; payloadShape × sendOutcome × retryProfile × postSendObservation = 3×3×3×3 = 81 combinations; hasMoreAfterSuccess = 2) → 810 distinct combinations. 220 runs covers 27% of the space by birthday-paradox argument; 150 would still catch the critical failures while saving ~30% of the Glados CI job time for this property. **Impact: ~30% faster Glados shard (saves ~10–15 s per CI build).**

- [ ] **[MED]** **Watchdog tests elapse 10 seconds of fake time** across 6+ tests in the `'watchdog'` group. Each `async.elapse(const Duration(seconds: 10))` is instant for `fakeAsync` itself, but the test still schedules microtasks for every timer tick in between. With `SyncTuning.outboxWatchdogInterval = 10s`, each elapse fires the watchdog once. These tests are correctly using `fakeAsync` so there is no real-time cost — but the watchdog group at lines 3368–3715 constructs 6 fresh `OutboxService` instances per test without any sharing. If `SyncTuning.outboxWatchdogInterval` ever changes, all 6 tests break silently. Extract a constant `_watchdogInterval` at the top of the test group and use it in all `async.elapse` calls. **Impact: maintainability.**

- [ ] **[LOW]** **`outbox_repository_test.dart` inner `'batch mark helpers'` group re-creates `realDb` in its own `setUp`/`tearDown` (lines 442–449) which shadows the outer `setUp` mock. Each real-DB test creates an in-memory SQLite database.** This is correct and necessary but could be documented with a comment so future authors don't merge the mock-level and real-DB tests.

---

## Summary

- **1 oversized implementation file** (`outbox_service.dart` at 1961 lines, ~4× guideline), with a clean split seam into an enqueue dispatcher + lifecycle controller.
- **2 oversized test files** (`outbox_service_test.dart` at 7743 lines, `outbox_processor_test.dart` at 1716 lines); the service test size is a direct consequence of the implementation not being split.
- **~10 weak/bloated test patterns**: 82 boilerplate-heavy service constructions with no `_TestBench`; 7 inline mock classes that should live in `test/mocks/mocks.dart`; inline GetIt wiring that bypasses `setUpTestGetIt()`; 5 static equality tests in `outbox_daily_volume_test.dart` that duplicate the Glados property; repeated Matrix-client setup boilerplate (~120 lines).
- **4 Glados tests, all properly tagged** — the only genuine Glados gap (VC merge idempotence) belongs in the `VectorClock` unit tests, not here.
- **3 meaningful coverage gaps**: missing positive assertion for `notLoggedInGateStream` emission post-grace-window; no real-DB test for the non-chunked `pruneSentOutboxItems`; untested `delay < Duration.zero` branch of `computeEnqueueDelay`.
- **2 speed wins**: 1 real-wall-time wait (200 ms, flake risk on slow CI); Glados `numRuns: 220` could drop to ~150 without coverage loss (~10–15 s saved per Glados CI job).
- **Biggest single opportunity**: split `outbox_service.dart` into an enqueue-dispatch class and a lifecycle class. This alone unblocks splitting the 7743-line test file, lets each half reach reasonable size, and allows the enqueue-side tests to run without Matrix/connectivity/timer setup.
